
# Glossary

AI - Artificial Intelligence

BERT – Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers

CRISP-DM - Cross-industry standard process for data mining

DPIA - Data Privacy Impact Assessment

iXBRL – Inline eXtensible Business Reporting Language

GDPR – General Data Protection Regulation

LLM - Large Language Model

ML – Machine learning

NN – Neural Network

RACI – Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed

RAG – Retrieval augmented generation

RNN – Recurrent Neural Network

SAS – Statistical Analysis System(Previously)

UX – User Experience

XBRL – eXtensible Business Reporting Language

# Notes

RACI

| ----------------------------------------- | ---------------- | ------------ | ----------------- | --------------- | ----------- | ----------------------- | --------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------- | --------- |
| Task                                      | Cabinet Minister | Project Lead | Platform Engineer | Data Scientists | UX Designer | Security and Compliance | Cloud Providers | External ML Experts | Subject matter experts | End Users |
| Project objectives                        | A                | R            | I                 | I               | I           | I                       | I               | I                   | C                      | C         |
| Business case & funding                   | A                | R            | C                 | C               | c           | C                       | I               | I                   | C                      | I         |
| Requirements                              | I                | A            | I                 | R               | R           | C                       | C               | C                   | C                      | I         |

| Setup infrastructure                      | I                | A            | R                 | C               | C           | C                       | R               | C                   | I                      | I         |
| Data ingestion and cleaning               | I                | A            | C                 | R               | I           | C                       | I               | C                   | C                      | I         |
| RAG Development                           | I                | A            | C                 | R               | C           | C                       | C               | C                   | C                      | I         |
| User interface design                     | I                | A            | I                 | C               | R           | C                       | I               | I                   | C                      | I         |
| Pilot deployment                          | I                | A            | R                 | C               | C           | C                       | C               | C                   | C                      | I         |
| Testing & evaluation                      | I                | A            | C                 | R               | R           | C                       | I               | C                   | R                      | R         |
| Full rollout to civil servants and public | A                | A            | R                 | C               | C           | C                       | C               | C                   | C                      | I         |


Company accounts are in a format called iXBRL, a (x)html document where figures are tagged with a fixed taxonomy. This allows for the data to be easily and accurately extracted from the accounts. But the tagging quality can be poor, in some cases over 70% of the figures are untagged. This can make it hard to extract and analyse this data. Descriptions and their values can otherwise be extracted but descriptions can vary greatly making profiling across them onerous and error prone.

We will use supervised ML to classify the descriptions, which will allow users to profile across data more reliably even when the data is not tagged, replacing long and complicated text matching. This would result in clearer, higher quality profiles that would mean we would be able to better target high risk companies for tax investigations increasing tax revenue and reduce investigations into low-risk companies, reducing customer burden.

Since the ML category might not always be true and accurate and ML isn’t always explainable, we should not be investigating into anyone solely based on the ML category. We need with a human in the loop, to ensure fairness and accountability.

There are legal and ethical constraints. While company accounts are primarily about corporates, there might be personal data in the accounts (e.g. company names, directors). This means that an appropriate DPIAs should be undertaken to ensure GDPR and other corporate compliance. This includes ensuring there are appropriate safeguards against bias and misuse.

## 2.2 Technical Approach

Accounts have their own terminology which can be quite specific and unique. Using a foundation model in a zero shot manner may work in generic tasks (Rothman, 2024, p215), but it won’t work well for accountancy terminology. The iXBRL taxonomies for the accounts can be very large, with millions of different descriptions used. So, providing examples in a prompt to a LLM isn’t going to be practical or feasible.

The iXBRL taxonomies contain a concept(XBRL tag/label) and a description. Simple approaches like cosine similarity(SpaCy) between the descriptions in the accounts and the descriptions in the taxonomies failed because the accounts have specific terminology that pretrained models struggle with. So, it’s important to train or fine-tune the model we use (Rothman, 2024, p142).  

This means that we need a large dataset to train or fine-tune a model against. It would take excessive amounts of work to manually tag it ourselves, and it would be hard to outsource it to something like Amazon Mechanical Turk, due to the technical knowledge required. Commercial software can extract XBRL tags from accounts, but don’t extract the descriptions or other untagged data. We will need to extract the data ourselves to train the model. Where values are tagged in the accounts, we can extract the descriptions with the associated XBRL tag(label) and use them to fine-tune a model in line with figure 2 below.

Traditional machine learning techniques such as Naïve Bayes and Random Forrest will work but a more modern LLM based model is better for understanding and classifying text.

We would want to process the data within a VPC, running the LLM on an EC2 instance, rather than using an API for security reasons. Given server costs and availability, we can’t use the latest and biggest LLMs available. A balance would be using a LLM such as a BERT that can run on existing servers.

A BERT model can be trained using the data in line with figure 2 below.

![A diagram of a diagram
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 1 Process flow to fine-tune BERT model

Once the model is trained it will be used to classify descriptions in line with figure 3 below.

![A diagram of a diagram
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 2 Process flow to categorise descriptions

The project will be done using CRISP-DM

1.    Business understanding. Understanding of company accounts, what’s missing and how to best exploit that data.

2.    Data understanding. iXBRL formatted documents and taxonomies.

3.    Data preparation. Extract data and clean data from iXBRL documents

4.    Modelling. Test hyperparameters and model(s).

5.    Evaluation. Use metrics and human evaluation.

6.    Deployment. Integrate the model with existing system and update processes to ensure humans-in-the loop are used when using the ML categories.

Full project resources, figure 4 below.

·      AWS Lambda downloading the accounts from companies house website once a day

·      AWS S3 bucket to store the accounts

·      EC2 instance to host the POSIT development environment with R/Python where the data is extracted and ML is run and then outputs saved on an

·      Oracle database. To store the outputs.

·      User tools. End users would be data analysts who exploit the data in Oracle using SAS/R/Python.

![A diagram of a company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company'
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 3 Architecture 

Full human resources

·      Platform engineer, manage the platforms and databases.

·      Developers, develop lambda functionality which gets the documents from companies house and saves it to S3

·      Data scientist, data preparation and ML development.

·      Analysts, will be the end users, they will test the outputs and provide feedback.


## 2.3 Artifact Design and Development

Sample data was obtained from Companies House (https://download.companieshouse.gov.uk/en_accountsdata.html). All the accounts submitted on 2025-06-21 was chosen as dataset large enough to give meaningful results, while still having reasonable performance. Once the process is finalised a larger dataset could improve model quality.

BeautifulSoup was used to extract the data from the iXBRL accounts since they are (x)html documents. Descriptions were extracted as the first text values in the line and the XBRL tags were the “name” attributes in an “ix:nonfraction” node.

Data exploration of the 15,362 lines of data involved functions like describe(), head() and frequency graphs, to help understand the data, check its validity and determine what cleaning and manipulation is required, appendix D.

Analysis showed descriptions were more varied than XBRL tags (frequency plots in figure 5 below), with mean counts of 4.8 and 42.8 respectively. With 3,207 unique descriptions which map to 359 unique XBRL tags, since people can describe things in different ways vs the fixed taxonomy for XBRL tags. Which means training is suitable.

![A graph of a number of frequency
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 1

We cleaned the data for various reasons such as preserving privacy, reducing noise and standardising the text, by converting to lowercase; replacing names, companies, numbers and dates with placeholders. Descriptions just of pure numbers or over ten words were dropped. Example of cleaned descriptions and XBRL tags(labels) in appendix C.

The cleaned dataset was split into a train and test dataset. This is so that we test the model on unseen data, preventing overfitting. The cleaned descriptions are then tokenised(bert-base-uncased), while the XBRL tags(labels) are encoded(LabelEncoder), then used to fine-tune BERT(bert-base-uncasd), (figure 2 in section 2.2)(code in appendix E).

After the model was trained various metrics were used, including accuracy, precision, recall and f1 to help evaluate it. Weighted averages were used since it will be applied over data with a similar weighting. While no one metric is “best”, it’s useful to look at the different metrics to see strengths and weaknesses.

Test accuracy increased from 0.79 in the first epoch to 0.86 after the fifth epoch, with the training and validation loss curves flatten around epoch 4, figure 6 below.

![A graph of training and validation loss
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 2

Actual results from fifth epoch.

Test Accuracy: 0.8566953797963978

Test Precision: 0.8562550207896077

Test Recall: 0.8566953797963978

Test F1: 0.8434924815875043

These scores are promising for a proof of concept. There are similar scores when tested on accounts from a different period, verifying that it works for completely unseen data.

Inspection of the outputs (appendix F) highlighted challenges. Most predictions are correct but the description of “Additions” is incorrectly characterised as  “AdditionsOtherThanThroughBusinessCombinationsPropertyPlantEquipment” when it was actually “TotalAdditionsIncludingFromBusinessCombinationsPropertyPlantEquipment”. Both categories are plausible and there isn’t enough information to determine which category it should be. 

Confusion matrixes showing that for some categories, like NetAssetsLiabilities (figure 7 below), it does very well, with only a single false positive, but others like PropertyPlantEquipment (figure 8 below), have 41 false negatives. Reviewing examples of incorrect results (appendix H), the descriptions incorrectly categorised as PropertyPlantEquipment are dates, which were standardised by a placeholder. Even the full date wouldn’t have enough information to accurately categorise those descriptions. A future improvement could be to remove dates in the cleaning phase.

![A screenshot of a graph
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 3

![A screenshot of a graph
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)


Figure 4

We demonstrated that fine-tuned BERT can accurately classify descriptions into taxonomy tags. It wasn’t perfect but reduces the need for manual text matching and has promise when improvements are made and larger datasets are used.

ML outputs are stored in an Oracle database available to limited number of analysts accessible through R and Python. Most analysts are GUI based using SAS which will require copying data over to that system to open up the data to the full analytical community.

The main user of this data would be analysts who want to profile across the untagged/unstructured part of company accounts. Previously they would need to do complicated text matching, which is error prone and time consuming. It is very hard to capture all the different variations and stemming, e.g. royalties could be described as “royalty paid”, “advances” and “license fees”. The ML category streamlines the process, allowing them to filter the ML category, in this example for royalties which is quicker and often more reliable.

Using the XBRL tags as categories means analysts are familiar with them and it more closely matches their existing workflow using XBRL tags. Fewer analysts are experienced using complex string matching. For those without deep tax knowledge, they can profile across the category of “equity”, without needing to know it might be described as “Retained earnings”.

Users have been involved in testing ML outputs as part of a larger project. They are testing every iteration and providing feedback both written and as a video call. They found that for certain ML categories they had to manually add filters on the descriptions to remove false positives or not use certain categories at all. But overall the time saved outweighed costs and they preferred using the ML categories.

We will need to embed the importance of human-in-the-loop for the ML category especially since the field is similar to the iXBRL field but unlike that it shouldn’t be used to make any automated decisions.

When we have rolled it out to the wider team, we will email them asking them to note down any issues they have, are any concepts particularly bad, or details of any other issues or what worked well and asking them to provide feedback anytime in the next 45 days.  

While various metrics can provide good insight into how well the product is doing, getting direct feedback from analysts that are using the ML categories can provide useful insights. For example, since the metrics are weighted, it might be that the model works well for common concepts but not that well on uncommonly used concepts, so human feedback is important.

# MLCC

Sample data was obtained from Companies House (https://download.companieshouse.gov.uk/en_accountsdata.html). A sample of 11,291 accounts submitted on 2025-08-01 were chosen as source documents containing data that can be extracted and trained upon. There are 140,685 lines of extracted data which is sufficient for reliable model quality and good performance. An example of company accounts in figure 5 below.

Company accounts and other iXBRL documents have details about the companies financial position and how they arrived at the figure of tax due. These can be used to help understand the economy for policy decision and can be used to highlight tax risk.

BeautifulSoup was used to extract the data from the iXBRL accounts since they are (x)html documents. Descriptions were extracted as the first text values in the line and the XBRL tags were the “name” attributes in an “ix:nonfraction” node, example of the document (figure 6 below) The aim is to extract the description and predict the XBRL tag associated with it.

Data is explored using function like describe(), head(), subpopulations and frequency graphs, to help understand the data, check its validity and determine what cleaning and manipulation is required (appendix C).

Analysis showed descriptions were more varied than XBRL tags (frequency plots in figure 7 below), with mean frequencies of 8.4 and 256.3 respectively. With 16,782 unique descriptions which map to 549 unique XBRL tags, since people can describe things in different ways vs the fixed taxonomy for XBRL tags. Which means the dataset is suitable for training.

The code for this proof of concept only extracts data if a html table is used. Some accounting software might be more preferred to smaller accountant than larger ones, meaning that the data might not be representative. There are some very long descriptions, means the logic in the extraction code isn’t always correct. There is no specific way to describe some items, so there might be a class and cultural bias in the descriptions used, that the model could exacerbate.

Data Preparation, clean the data to preserve privacy, reducing noise and standardising the text. Converting to lowercase; replacing names, companies, numbers and dates with placeholders. Drop pure numbers, pure dates, pure names or strings over ten words to remove noise and improve model efficiency. The data is then split into train, validation and test, with validation sets being used for hyperparameter tuning and the test set being used to evaluate generalisation and detect overfitting (appendix C).

TextVectorization(Keras) is used to tokenisation by splitting by whitespace and then mapped to integer indices (appendix C). This doesn’t capture any learning or meaning behind the words or phrases, but that is captured in the embedding step of the DNN. The advantage being that embedding is trained to be specific for this use case rather then just generic word embeddings. Labels are encoded using LabelEncoder(scikit-learn).

![A diagram of a diagram
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 1 The pipeline for tokenising and vectorising the descriptions, encoding the labels, which are fed into the NN for training.

Multiple DNN such as CNN, RNN/LSTM/GRU, will be tried during training using a Kera API pipeline. Different layer types, activation functions and dropouts are evaluated to access their impact on performance. Hyperparameters optimisation done using the validation dataset.

Final model evaluation will be using the unseen test dataset, testing across accuracy, precision, recall, f1 score. For imbalanced data sets macro f1 scores can be more informative than accuracy. Review of some of the confusion matrices for specific topics and also review examples of matches and mismatches. To gain insight into the strengths and weaknesses of the model.

While the CPU could be the cheapest other factors like electricity use and CO2 emissions are important factors to consider. Generally, CPUs aren’t specialised and since they take longer to run they can use more electricity than a GPU.

It can be cost prohibitive to run a GPU instance running constantly. There is On-Demand Compute(ODC) where a POSIT environment on an EC2 instance with a GPU can take over 10 minutes to start up, for a specific purpose and then closed down when done. The long start up time can mean that it can be even more efficient to use CPU compared to GPU. For this use case it’s more cost and time effective to do inference existing CPU based system than using ODC with GPU. For training larger datasets GPUs may be preferential.

‌For security reasons due to the sensitive nature of the data it is covered by the Data Protection act and other organisation rules. There are DPIAs used to ensure the law and organisation rules are covered and risks mitigated and understood. This means that many API or fully managed services like SageMaker AI services, are unsuitable. Instead, systems that can be managed such as EC2 instances with Keras and TensorFlow are preferred.  Similarly, Google CoLab can only be used over public data and not the larger private datasets available. The simplicity of CoLab isn’t easily replicable on bespoke EC2 setups, with various issues preventing GPU usage.

There is also a desire to create systems that can work cross platform. All the major cloud providers will support a Keras workflow but only AWS would support SageMaker, resulting in a risk of being locked into AWS.

The application will help identify high risk companies that could have a tax investigations opened into them. It is important to have a human in the loop double checking any ML outputs, and that there shouldn’t be any investigations solely due to a ML category. It is possible that ML could propagate biases in the data or training data, so it’s important to look out for them and mitigate them as much as possible.

# Programming for AI

Presentation
